1.pericope_label: He: Further Praise of the Beloved
1.synthesis:
The third major wasf of the beloved is the most physically complete and most exuberantly appreciative of all the descriptions in the Song. It moves from feet to head — the reverse of the standard descending order — beginning with 'How beautiful are your feet in sandals, O noble daughter!' (v. 1) and ascending through thighs, navel, belly, breasts, neck, eyes, nose, head, and hair. The ascending direction (feet to head) creates an ascending energy: the wasf builds toward its climax rather than descending from it.

The comparison of the navel to 'a rounded bowl that never lacks mixed wine' (v. 2) and the belly to 'a heap of wheat, encircled with lilies' (v. 2) are among the more unusual comparisons of the Song: the navel as a cup never lacking wine suggests the intoxicating abundance the beloved offers; the wheat heap encircled with lilies is an agricultural image of abundance and beauty combined. The series continues with the two-fawns image for the breasts (v. 3, echoing 4:5), the ivory-tower nose, the pools-of-Heshbon eyes, the Lebanon-tower neck — each comparison grounding the beloved's features in the most impressive geographical and architectural features of the land.

The climactic declaration of vv. 6–9 moves from description to desire: 'How beautiful and pleasant you are, O loved one, with all your delights! Your stature is like a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters. I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its fruit. Oh may your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the scent of your breath like apples, and your mouth like the best wine.' The beloved's declaration that he will 'climb the palm tree and lay hold of its fruit' is the most direct expression of active desire in the entire wasf tradition. Love moves from aesthetic appreciation to physical pursuit — the description is not an end in itself but a preparation for the embrace it describes.
1.historical_context: The ascending wasf structure (feet to head) is unique in the Song's three extended wasfs — all others descend from head to body. The specific geographical references — tower of Lebanon, pools of Heshbon, gate of Bath-rabbim, Mount Carmel — locate the beloved within the landscape of Israel, making her beauty coextensive with the beauty of the land itself. Heshbon was a Transjordanian city; Bath-rabbim ('daughter of many') was one of its gates. Mount Carmel's head was the highest point on the coast — the comparison of the beloved's head to Carmel suggests her total visual dominance of the landscape she inhabits.
1.literary_structure: The wasf (vv. 1–5) leads into the transition from observation to desire (v. 6), the active pursuit declaration (vv. 7–8), and the sensory-climax of mouth-wine (v. 9a). The section ends with the woman's response beginning mid-verse at v. 9b — the transition is so seamless it is unclear where the beloved's speech ends and the woman's begins, suggesting the complete interpenetration of their voices and desires at this point in the book.
1.theological_application: The beloved's movement from describing beauty to actively pursuing it — 'I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its fruit' (v. 8) — is the Song's model of love as active, pursuing desire rather than passive appreciation. Love does not merely observe beauty from a distance but moves toward it, reaches for it, seeks to hold it. This active quality of love reflects the character of divine love throughout Scripture: YHWH does not merely observe Israel from a distance but comes down (Exod 3:8), sends (John 3:16), and pursues his people with the same active determination that the beloved shows in pursuing the one he loves.
1.christology: The beloved climbing the palm tree to hold its fruit — love made physically active, pursuing what it desires — is read in the Christian tradition as the incarnation: God climbing down into creation to hold what he loves, the Word becoming flesh to take hold of human nature in its particularity. The 'best wine' to which the beloved's mouth is compared (v. 9) echoes the wine of Cana (John 2:10) — 'you have kept the best wine until last' — the wine of the new covenant that Christ provides as the fullest expression of love's abundance, surpassing all that came before.

10.synthesis:
The third and final version of the mutual-belonging formula in v. 10 is the most radical: 'I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me.' The formula has undergone a significant transformation through the Song. In 2:16: 'My beloved is mine and I am his' — equal mutuality, his possession of her stated first. In 6:3: 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' — still mutual, but her belonging to him stated first. Now in 7:10: 'I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me' — the second clause has entirely changed. Instead of 'my beloved is mine,' it is 'his desire is toward me.'

The word 'desire' (tĕšûqāh) is theologically charged: it appears in only two other places in the OT. In Gen 3:16, it describes the woman's desire for the man as part of the curse; in Gen 4:7, it describes sin's desire for Cain. In Song 7:10, the word is used of the man's desire for the woman — a reversal of the Genesis 3:16 curse. The beloved's desire (tĕšûqāh) turned toward her is the redemption of what the curse distorted: love's desire restored to its created mutuality, the man's desire directed toward the woman without domination.

The invitation of vv. 11–13 — 'Come, my beloved, let us go out into the fields and lodge in the villages; let us go out early to the vineyards and see whether the vines have budded... there I will give you my love' — reverses the directional dynamic of the Song: rather than the beloved coming to the woman, she now invites him to come with her to the countryside. The outdoors, the garden, the vineyard — the spaces of creation and agricultural labor — are the setting she chooses for love's fulfillment. The mandrakes of v. 13, traditionally believed to promote fertility, add a note of covenantal fruitfulness: this love desires not merely pleasure but generativity.
10.historical_context: The tĕšûqāh (desire) of v. 10 is a theologically loaded word that appears only three times in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its appearances in Gen 3:16 (the woman's desire for the man as curse-consequence) and Gen 4:7 (sin's desire for Cain) give the Song's use of the same word for the man's desire toward the woman a redemptive resonance: the curse's distortion of desire is being reversed in this love relationship. The mandrakes (dûdāʾîm) of v. 13 were a plant associated with aphrodisiac and fertility properties in the ancient Near East (cf. Gen 30:14–16).
10.literary_structure: The section's movement from the belonging declaration (v. 10) to outdoor invitation (vv. 11–12) to the love-offer in the vineyard (vv. 12b–13) traces an arc from the theological to the experiential: the declaration of belonging leads to the invitation to come, which leads to the offer of love in the place of beauty and work combined. The vineyard as the setting of love is both echo of the vineyards the beloved was forced to tend (1:6) and the site of her own choice — she now chooses the vineyard as the place of freely offered love.
10.theological_application: The transformation of the tĕšûqāh formula — from the curse of Gen 3:16 (the woman's desire producing a power-struggle) to the restoration of Song 7:10 (the man's desire turned toward the woman without domination) — is the Song's contribution to the theology of marriage's redemption. What the fall corrupted — the mutuality of desire and self-giving — the covenant of love restores: he desires her, she belongs to him, and the desire is not domination but gift. This is the eschatological vision of restored love that the Song holds before the community as the goal and the gift of covenant love.
10.christology: The tĕšûqāh reversal — the divine beloved's desire turned toward his people — is the theological content of the incarnation. The God whose people turned away from him in the fall now turns his desire toward them: 'God so loved the world that he gave his only Son' (John 3:16). The 'desire' of the divine lover is the cross: 'for the joy that was set before him he endured the cross' (Heb 12:2). The 'joy set before him' is the union with his beloved bride — the church — which his passion made possible. His tĕšûqāh toward his people is the love that drives the entire redemptive narrative.
